evals.prompt: Audit this 14-bet portfolio. Bet 1: AI chatbot for customer service, in beta 11 months, no production launch. Bet 2: Mobile app redesign, launched 6 months ago, adoption flat. Bet 3: Voice-of-customer dashboard, in discovery 14 months, sponsor unclear. Bet 4: New B2B billing platform, in design phase 8 months. Bet 5: Marketplace for partner ecosystem, ideation 18 months, funded $2M with no progress. Bets 6-14 are similar UX/digital improvements with no clear outcome metrics. Tell me what to kill, advance, and merge.
evals.expected_output: Should map across H1/H2/H3 (most look H1 except Bet 5 which is H2/H3-shaped). Should identify stalled bets (>1.5x median in stage): Bet 1 (11mo in beta — almost certainly Gate 3 failure, scope or operating-model gap), Bet 3 (14mo in discovery — sponsor problem, recommend kill or sponsor reset), Bet 5 (18mo ideation, $2M spent, zero progress — clear kill, this is undead pattern). Recommend: KILL Bet 5 immediately, do kill ceremony, redeploy capital. KILL Bet 3 unless sponsor resets in 30 days. ADVANCE Bet 1 to launch with explicit Gate 3 criteria within 60 days, or kill. Bet 2 needs root-cause analysis — is the redesign solving a real problem? MERGE Bets 6-14 are likely overlapping; recommend consolidation to 3-5 strategically distinct bets. Note healthy kill rate is 30-50%; this portfolio has zero kills which means gates are toothless. Recommend gate redesign as follow-on.

evals.prompt: Design Gate 1 criteria for an H3 bet on AI-native customer onboarding for wealth management.
evals.expected_output: Should refuse to apply standard stage-gate criteria to H3 work. Recommend discovery-driven planning instead. Gate 1 (Discovery → Alpha) criteria: (1) ≥10 customer interviews confirming the problem (current onboarding pain quantified — drop-off rates, time-to-funded, advisor frustration), (2) job story written with circumstance map (wealth-management onboarding is a specific job, not generic), (3) competing solutions inventoried including doing nothing and competitor offerings, (4) the riskiest assumption identified — likely 'AI conversational interface produces higher completion than human advisor' or 'compliance can be embedded in AI flow without human checkpoint', (5) MVC concept tested with 5+ target users. Decision: fund Alpha (typically $250K-$500K for AI-native discovery work) or kill/pivot. Should NOT include revenue projections or NPV at Gate 1 — this is H3 territory.

evals.prompt: Our gates are toothless. Every bet advances. Redesign for a 30-50% kill rate.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose causes of toothless gates: (1) criteria too vague to fail, (2) sunk-cost bias overrides criteria, (3) sponsors save their bets, (4) no kill ceremony so kills feel like punishment, (5) reviewers lack authority. Recommendations: (1) Make criteria binary and observable — 'has X assumption been tested with N customers and produced Y signal' rather than 'is showing promise'; (2) Pre-mortem at funding — name the specific conditions under which we'd kill, ratify in writing, kill without re-litigation when conditions hit; (3) Build kill ceremonies that celebrate the team and the learning; (4) Stage SLAs — bets exceeding 1.5x median time in stage default to kill review; (5) Healthy-kill-rate target communicated to leaders (30-50%); (6) Quarterly zombie audit — bets 'almost ready' for 9+ months are killed automatically; (7) Reviewers must include peer practitioners with no direct stake. Note that toothless gates often correlate with capital-deficit leadership — sponsors don't have political cover to kill. Recommend pairing gate redesign with leadership-coach intervention.

evals.prompt: Build the quarterly portfolio review template for a $50M innovation budget across 22 bets.
evals.expected_output: Should output structured 2-3 hour QBR template: (1) Opening — portfolio health scorecard (20 min) — capital deployed by horizon, bets by stage, kill rate this quarter, learning velocity, advance/kill/pivot count; (2) Per-horizon deep dive (60-90 min) — H1 (15 min: business cases, NPV, NRR), H2 (30 min: pilots, unit economics, market validation), H3 (30 min: option value secured, capability built); (3) Decision agenda (30 min) — bets needing fund/advance/kill/pivot decisions, with pre-circulated value cases; (4) Reallocation (15 min) — capital flowing between bets based on quarter's learning; (5) New intake (10 min) — bets entering Gate 0; (6) Action items (10 min). Required pre-reads: portfolio dashboard, value cases for advance/kill decisions, post-mortems for kills. Note that 22 bets is heavy — recommend cap at 15-18 to keep QBR substantive vs. surface.

evals.prompt: We have pilot purgatory — 6 successful pilots that never launched. Diagnose Gate 3.
evals.expected_output: Should identify Gate 3 (Beta → Launch) as the failure point and surface common causes: (1) no scale-up funding pre-committed at Gate 2 — pilots succeed and then there's no money for launch; (2) no operating-BU owner identified — successful pilot has no home; (3) no handoff protocol — Lab/team owns the pilot but can't ship to BU; (4) no launch playbook — pilot was hand-crafted, scale requires productization; (5) pricing not validated — pricing surprises emerge at launch; (6) brand/positioning not designed — marketing isn't ready; (7) sales enablement gap — sales doesn't know how to sell. Recommendations: (1) at Gate 2, require pre-committed launch funding tier, named operating BU owner, and handoff protocol; (2) build a Launch Readiness Checklist as Gate 3 criteria; (3) include innovation-monetization-strategist for pricing validation at Gate 2 not Gate 3; (4) include cross-plugin handoffs (brand-strategist for positioning, marketing-agent for sales enablement). Note this is the most common Gate 3 failure pattern — pilot success creates false confidence about scale-readiness.

evals.prompt: How should I allocate $20M across H1/H2/H3 given a 'find next-S-curve' mandate? What's the staged-funding pattern within each horizon?
evals.expected_output: Default allocation for find-next-S-curve mandate: ~60/30/10 = $12M H1, $6M H2, $2M H3. Recommend hybrid model — fixed pool per horizon, performance-based advancement within. Within H3 ($2M total): plan for 4-6 discovery bets at $50-100K each ($300-600K), 2-3 advancing to alpha at $250-500K each ($750K-1.5M), 1-2 making it to beta. Within H2 ($6M): mix of new bets and continuation of validated discovery → 4-6 active bets, $250K-$1.5M each at various stages. Within H1 ($12M): 5-8 bets, $1-3M each, traditional stage-gate. Note kill capital should be ~15-30% of total ($3-6M) — that's healthy, not waste. Recommend quarterly rebalance based on learning. Caution: don't pre-commit all $2M H3 to one bet — staged funding across 4-6 discovery bets generates more option value than one big H3 wager.

evals.prompt: Build the portfolio dashboard for our weekly leadership review.
evals.expected_output: Should output single-page dashboard structure: (1) Portfolio Health Scorecard — bets by horizon, capital deployed/total, headcount; (2) Per-horizon health — green/yellow/red per active bet with 1-line status; (3) Velocity metrics — experiments per week (target 5-10), validation cycle time (target <14 days), bets advanced this week, bets killed this week; (4) Decision queue — bets needing executive decision in next 2 weeks; (5) Learning highlights — top 3 invalidated assumptions this week (these are valuable signals, not failures); (6) Capital efficiency — expected portfolio value / total deployed. Should specify update cadence (weekly, refreshed Monday AM) and ownership (portfolio architect or PMO). Should NOT include revenue metrics for H3 bets — those are wrong-horizon metrics. Should fit on single screen for tablet/phone executive viewing.

evals.prompt: What's a healthy kill rate, and how do I report it without it looking like failure?
evals.expected_output: Healthy kill rate at gates: 30-50%. Below 20%: gates are toothless, intake is too restrictive, or both — portfolio not taking enough risk. Above 70%: intake is too loose, discovery is thin, or strategy is poorly defined. Reframe kill rate as portfolio discipline indicator, not failure indicator. Reporting language: 'We killed [N] bets this quarter, freeing $[X] for higher-conviction opportunities. Kill rate of [Y]% is in our healthy range of 30-50%, indicating gate criteria are working.' Pair with capital-recycled metric ($ from killed bets reallocated to advancing bets). Pair with learning-from-kills metric (top 3 things we learned that we'll apply elsewhere). Critical: kill ceremonies should celebrate the team and the learning — without ritual, kills hide and zombie bets accumulate. Reference Catmull/Pixar pattern of postmortem as ritual not investigation.
